narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped, landing at a 45% chance of not passing. Picture your profile coming together like chapters in a story: your Year 3 grades placing you in the 3rd decile stood out as the biggest signal, whispering that the rigors of upper-level law courses might prove an uphill battle, much like they've done for others in similar spots. Right behind that was your LSAT score of 28, which, while showing real grit, fell short of the benchmarks that typically light the way to bar success for students like you. Your Year 1 decile of 3 layered on early confirmation that building momentum in a demanding program could be tough from the start. Even your upper-middle family income, which offers a solid safety net for many, didn't quite offset the patterns we've seen where it hasn't always bridged gaps in academic performance here. Your Hispanic background, female perspective, undergraduate GPA of 2.5, and full-time study plans each tugged gently in the same direction, painting a composite picture of someone facing steeper odds against the bar's intense demands. It's not that any one piece defined you—it's this thoughtful blend that made the model pause, urging us to protect spots for profiles with smoother predicted paths. You've got undeniable strengths, like committing to full-time focus, and this isn't the end of your story; many have turned similar starting points into triumphs with targeted boosts. What if you revisited LSAT prep or sought mentorship tailored to thriving in law school? We're rooting for you to rewrite this narrative on your terms.
